Abstract:
Η αδυναμία των συνοχικών κόνεων να ρέουν σταθερά και αξιόπιστα αποτελεί κύρια αιτία διακοπής λειτουργίας και απώλειας προϊόντων σε ένα ευρύ φάσμα βιομηχανιών επεξεργασίας σκονών. Έχει πραγματοποιηθεί εκτεταμένη έρευνα για τον χαρακτηρισμό της ρευστότητας των σκονών χρησιμοποιώντας μια ευρεία γκάμα τεχνικών, με την πιο εδραιωμένη μέθοδο μέτρησης της ρευστότητας σκονών σε μέτριες έως υψηλές τάσεις να είναι τα κυψελοειδή εργαλεία διάτμησης, με θεωρίες που έχουν αναπτυχθεί για τον σχεδιασμό σιλό και χοανών. Ωστόσο, πολλές βιομηχανικές διαδικασίες μεγάλου ενδιαφέροντος, όπως η πλήρωση και δοσομέτρηση σκονών σε κάψουλες και η διασπορά σε εισπνευστήρες ξηρών σκονών (DPI), εκθέτουν τα σωματίδια σε πολύ χαμηλές τάσεις συμπύκνωσης (≤ 1 kPa), όπου ο προσδιορισμός της ασυγκράτητης αντοχής διάρρηξης μέσω δοκιμών διάτμησης συχνά παρουσιάζει ασυνέπειες στη μέτρηση ή σε σύγκριση με την παρατηρούμενη συμπεριφορά. Επομένως, υπάρχει ανάγκη για μια εναλλακτική προσέγγιση για την κατανόηση της ρευστότητας των ασθενώς συμπυκνωμένων σκονών, όπως η μέθοδος εμβάπτισης με μπάλα, η οποία είναι μια τεχνική διείσδυσης που μπορεί να αξιολογήσει τη ρευστότητα των σκονών μέχρι πολύ χαμηλές τάσεις, ενώ απαιτεί μόνο μια πολύ μικρή ποσότητα υλικού. Σε αυτήν την τεχνική, ένα κρεβάτι σκόνης συμπυκνώνεται σε οποιαδήποτε επιθυμητή τάση που παρέχει μια σχετικά επίπεδη επιφάνεια και στη συνέχεια διεισδύεται από έναν σφαιρικό διαβρωτή. Η ρευστότητα προσδιορίζεται με τον υπολογισμό της σκληρότητας του κρεβατιού σκόνης, από την απόκριση δύναμης-μετατόπισης του κρεβατιού. Η σκληρότητα μπορεί να συσχετιστεί με την ασυγκράτητη αντοχή διάρρηξης, που συνήθως προκύπτει μέσω των δοκιμών διάτμησης, μέσω του συντελεστή περιορισμού, ο οποίος εξαρτάται από τις ιδιότητες των σωματιδίων, αν και δεν μπορεί ακόμα να προσδιοριστεί εκ των προτέρων. Σε αυτήν την εργασία, ο συντελεστής περιορισμού μιας ευρείας κλάσης σκονών ποσοτικοποιήθηκε μέσω πειραμάτων εμβάπτισης και κυψελών διάτμησης σε μέτριες έως υψηλές τάσεις και διαπιστώθηκε ότι γενικά είναι ανεξάρτητος από την εφαρμοζόμενη τάση, ενώ για τέσσερα από τα είκοσι πέντε υλικά παρουσίασε διακυμάνσεις. Για να εξαχθεί η ασυγκράτητη αντοχή διάρρηξης από τις μετρήσεις σκληρότητας σε χαμηλές τάσεις, υποτέθηκε ότι ο συντελεστής περιορισμού παραμένει σταθερός σε χαμηλότερες τάσεις. Χρησιμοποιήθηκε επίσης η Μοντελοποίηση Διακριτών Στοιχείων (DEM) για τη προσομοίωση του συστήματος εμβάπτισης με μπάλα, επιτρέποντας τον προσδιορισμό των εσωτερικών τάσεων διάρρηξης του κρεβατιού σκόνης προκειμένου να διασαφηνιστεί η συμπεριφορά του συντελεστή περιορισμού σε χαμηλές τάσεις. Οι προσομοιώσεις επιβεβαίωσαν την υπόθεση ότι ο συντελεστής περιορισμού παραμένει σταθερός καθ' όλη τη διάρκεια του φάσματος εφαρμοζόμενων τάσεων.Επιπλέον, η εφαρμοσιμότητα και αξιοπιστία τόσο της εμβάπτισης με μπάλα όσο και της κυψέλης διάτμησης FT4 αξιολογήθηκαν σε ένα ευρύ φάσμα τόσο χαμηλών όσο και μέτριων έως υψηλών τάσεων. Η εμβάπτιση με μπάλα έδωσε πολύ επαναλήψιμα αποτελέσματα σε ολόκληρο το φάσμα των εφαρμοζόμενων τάσεων, ενώ η κυψέλη διάτμησης FT4 κρίθηκε αναξιόπιστη για τα περισσότερα υλικά σε κανονικές τάσεις προδιάτμησης 1 kPa και κάτω. Για όλα τα υλικά, εκτός από τις τρεις σκόνες που παραμένουν πολύ συνοχικές καθ' όλη τη διάρκεια του φάσματος τάσεων που δοκιμάστηκε, παρατηρήθηκε ότι η αύξηση της σκληρότητας (και συνεπώς και της ασυγκράτητης αντοχής διάρρηξης που προκύπτει από την εμβάπτιση με μπάλα) με την τάση ήταν πολύ πιο απότομη σε χαμηλές τάσεις, σε σύγκριση με υψηλότερες τάσεις, λόγω της πιο γρήγορης αύξησης της πυκνότητας συσκευασίας. Για όλα τα μοντέλα γυάλινων σφαιριδίων που δοκιμάστηκαν, εκτός από τα δείγματα 0 - 20 μm, η σκληρότητα βρέθηκε να είναι ανεξάρτητη από το βάθος διείσδυσης σε ένα συγκεκριμένο εύρος βάθους. Αντίθετα, για τα περισσότερα 'πραγματικά' υλικά, καθώς και για τα προαναφερθέντα πολύ λεπτά γυάλινα σφαιρίδια μοντέλου, η σκληρότητα βρέθηκε να αυξάνεται συνεχώς με το βάθος, με μια κλίση που είναι ανεξάρτητη από την εφαρμοζόμενη τάση και παρόμοια για όλα τα υλικά που δοκιμάστηκαν. Οι σκόνες που είναι επιρρεπείς σε ασταθή ροή (stick-slip) αποκλίνουν από την παραπάνω συμπεριφορά και εμφάνισαν μια κυμαινόμενη απόκριση δύναμης. Η επίδραση μιας ποικιλίας ιδιοτήτων σωματιδίων στον συντελεστή περιορισμού και συνεπώς στη ρευστότητα της σκόνης προσδιορίστηκε τόσο πειραματικά όσο και υπολογιστικά. Οι επιδράσεις του μεγέθους των σωματιδίων, της κατανομής του μεγέθους, και του σχήματος μεμονωμένων σωματιδίων και συσσωματωμάτων μελετήθηκαν πειραματικά, ενώ η στατική και κυλιόμενη τριβή, καθώς και η πλαστική τάση διάρρηξης μεταβλήθηκαν στις προσομοιώσεις DEM. Η επίδραση της επιφανειακής ενέργειας μελετήθηκε τόσο πειραματικά όσο και με τη μοντελοποίηση DEM. Όλες οι ιδιότητες βρέθηκαν να επηρεάζουν τον συντελεστή περιορισμού και τη συμπεριφορά ροής σε κάποιο βαθμό, υποδεικνύοντας την πολυπλοκότητα της συμπεριφοράς ροής των σκονών.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000013

From text 'rehrl2018controlofthree pages 1-4'.

Jakob Rehrl, Anssi-Pekka Karttunen, Niels Nicolaï, Theresa Hörmann, Martin Horn, Ossi Korhonen, Ingmar Nopens, Thomas De Beer, and Johannes G. Khinast. Control of three different continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing processes: use of soft sensors. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 543:60–72, May 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027. This article has 85 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The paper develops mathematical models for continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing that predict API concentration from feeder data using soft sensor approaches. The models are based on non-ideal mixing models incorporating tank-in-series (CSTR) components combined with plug flow/delay-time elements. Transfer functions are used rather than convolution integrals for analyzing interconnected systems. The work experimentally determines residence time distributions across three different continuous processing lines: hot melt extrusion (HME), direct compaction (DC), and wet granulation (WG). These dynamic models capture the residence time distribution of blending steps and are designed to be directly applicable in control strategies. The soft sensors use API concentration computed from gravimetric feeder data to predict actual API concentration after blending, potentially replacing or serving as redundancy for NIR spectroscopy measurements.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 1-2'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

For continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing feeder and blender systems, residence time distribution (RTD) models are used to investigate material traceability and process dynamics. The primary process models employed are tank-in-series (TIS) and axial dispersion models, which are imported from liquid operations and have proven effective for solid operations. These models often incorporate dead time implementation to describe the plug flow region where materials travel without back mixing. RTD is typically measured through impulse or step disturbance tracer experiments, which determine probability distribution functions (PDF) or cumulative distribution functions (CDF). The article specifically addresses the challenge that many unit operations in the pharmaceutical industry are characterized by dead time-dominated RTD. Advanced feedback control structures, such as Smith predictors implementing RTD, are used to manage the control of these systems.

## pqac-00000015

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 12-13'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt provides references to multiple modeling approaches for continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing feeder and blending systems. Key models identified include: (1) Residence Time Distribution (RTD) models used for characterizing continuous powder mixing and blending processes; (2) Artificial neural networks combined with RTD models for digital twins of low dosage continuous powder blending; (3) Periodic section modeling for optimizing continuous powder mixing; (4) RTD-based feeding models for process design; (5) RTD modeling approaches for monitoring drug concentration in tablet press feed frames, specifically addressing dead zones; and (6) RTD-based flowsheet modeling frameworks for assessing in-process control strategies. The excerpt indicates these models are applied to address material traceability, characterize mixing behavior, and handle dead time-dominated systems in continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing, though specific technical details of the models are not provided in this section.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'sacher2020towardsanovel pages 9-13'.

Stephan Sacher, Selma Celikovic, Jakob Rehrl, Johannes Poms, Martin Kirchengast, Julia Kruisz, Martin Sipek, Sharareh Salar-Behzadi, Hannes Berger, Gerald Stark, Martin Horn, and Johannes G. Khinast. Towards a novel continuous hme-tableting line: process development and control concept. European Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 142:105097, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejps.2019.105097, doi:10.1016/j.ejps.2019.105097. This article has 29 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes process modeling approaches for a continuous hot-melt-extrusion tableting line. For the extruder and blender units, Residence Time Distribution (RTD) models based on transfer functions were developed, with parameters identified through impulse response tests using color tracer methodology. Dead time between the die plate and pelletizer outlet was determined experimentally using a stopwatch. Cooling and pelletization processes were modeled using the local linear model tree (LOLIMOT) algorithm, which employs multiple local linear models with input variables including die plate temperature, cooling air pressure, and intake speed. The excerpt also mentions chemometric models developed using partial least squares (PLS) for API concentration prediction via near-infrared spectroscopy. However, the excerpt does not specifically address dedicated feeder system models or provide detailed mathematical formulations of the RTD or dynamic process models themselves.

## pqac-00000017

From text 'rehrl2018controlofthree pages 4-8'.

Jakob Rehrl, Anssi-Pekka Karttunen, Niels Nicolaï, Theresa Hörmann, Martin Horn, Ossi Korhonen, Ingmar Nopens, Thomas De Beer, and Johannes G. Khinast. Control of three different continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing processes: use of soft sensors. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 543:60–72, May 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027. This article has 85 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the application of transfer function models (based on Ogata, 2004) to capture residence time distribution in continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing processes. Rather than using convolution integrals in the time domain, the authors employ transfer functions to compute interconnected CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) systems through multiplication, which simplifies computation and system behavior analysis. The approach is validated across three manufacturing lines: hot-melt extrusion, direct compression, and wet granulation. For each process, residence time distribution was experimentally measured using tracer materials (Fe2O3 for extrusion, Paracetamol for compression, external microfeeder for granulation) inserted at inlet points with measurements captured at outlet locations via NIR spectroscopy. The modeling approach accounts for delay time and is tested at multiple mass flow rates (typically three different rates per process) to demonstrate applicability across whole operation windows rather than single operating points.

URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejps.2019.105097, doi:10.1016/j.ejps.2019.105097. This article has 29 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes modeling approaches for continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing. For cooling and pelletization, the Local Linear Model Tree (LOLIMOT) algorithm is employed, consisting of multiple local linear models with inputs including die plate temperature, cooling air pressure, and pelletizer intake speed. The models output strand thickness and strand temperature. Residence Time Distribution (RTD) profiles are determined through color-based analysis, assuming linear relationships between color intensity and concentration. Dead time is measured experimentally using a stopwatch. An RTD model of the feeding blending unit is utilized for process control decisions. The control concept implements PID controllers for API concentration in the extruder and blend homogeneity, along with Model Predictive Control (MPC) for tablet press hopper levels and mass flow rates. However, the excerpt does not provide detailed specifications of feeder-specific dynamic models or comprehensive descriptions of dedicated feeder modeling approaches.

## pqac-00000019

From text 'rehrl2018controlofthree pages 18-22'.

Jakob Rehrl, Anssi-Pekka Karttunen, Niels Nicolaï, Theresa Hörmann, Martin Horn, Ossi Korhonen, Ingmar Nopens, Thomas De Beer, and Johannes G. Khinast. Control of three different continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing processes: use of soft sensors. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 543:60–72, May 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027. This article has 85 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes soft sensor models developed for continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing that predict API (active pharmaceutical ingredient) content at intermediate product positions. These models are based on residence time distribution (RTD) of unit operations and use a unified framework applicable to different manufacturing line types (DC, WG, and HME lines mentioned). The soft sensors utilize feeder mass flow data as input to predict API concentration without requiring additional measurements beyond timely-resolved feeder data. The models can account for varying mass flows by interpolating parameters between throughput values used in tracer experiments. Key advantages include cost savings compared to NIR equipment, capability to detect future API concentration changes, and straightforward implementation in industrial SCADA systems. The approach demonstrates that one model structure can capture RTDs across different unit operations despite varying time scales, and allows predicting material discharge decisions in advance.

    author = "Jones-Salkey, Owen and Chu, Zoe and Ingram, Andrew and Windows-Yule, Christopher R. K.",

    year = "2023",
    journal = "Pharmaceutics",
    volume = "15",

    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "6",
    issn = "1999-4923"
}


Abstract:
The pharmaceutical industry is undergoing a paradigm shift towards continuous processing from batch, where continuous direct compression (CDC) is considered to offer the most straightforward implementation amongst powder processes due to the relatively low number of unit operations or handling steps. Due to the nature of continuous processing, the bulk properties of the formulation will require sufficient flowability and tabletability in order to be processed and transported effectively to and from each unit operation. Powder cohesion presents one of the greatest obstacles to the CDC process as it inhibits powder flow. As a result, there have been many studies investigating potential manners in which to overcome the effects of cohesion with, to date, little consideration of how these controls may affect downstream unit operations. The aim of this literature review is to explore and consolidate this literature, considering the impact of powder cohesion and cohesion control measures on the three-unit operations of the CDC process (feeding, mixing, and tabletting). This review will also cover the consequences of implementing such control measures whilst highlighting subject matter which could be of value for future research to better understand how to manage cohesive powders for CDC manufacture.

Abstract:
Solid-state fermentation (SSF) system involves solid, liquid, and gas phases, characterized by complex mass and heat transfer mechanisms and microbial complex interactions. The SSF processes for traditional Chinese fermented foods, such as vinegar, soy sauce, and baijiu primarily rely on experience, and most of the operations are replaced by auto machine now. However, there is still a lack of engineering in-depth study of the microbial process of SSF for complete process control. To meet the demands of smart manufacturing and green production, this paper emphasizes the engineering analysis of the mechanisms behind SSF. It reviews the progress in the engineering aspects of Chinese traditional SSF, including raw material pretreatment, process parameter detection, mathematical model construction, and equipment innovation. Additionally, it summarizes the challenges faced during intelligent upgrades and the opportunities brought by scientific and technological advancements, proposing future development directions. This review provides an overview of the SSF engineering aspects, offering a reference for the intelligent transformation and sustainable development of the Chinese traditional SSF food industry.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Additive manufacturing (AM) is increasingly recognized as a critical enabler for sustainable space exploration, offering on-demand fabrication, reduced reliance on Earth-based resupply, and enhanced mission autonomy. Over the past decade, in-space AM has progressed from early polymer extrusion experiments aboard the International Space Station (ISS) to the demonstration of multi-material capabilities involving polymers, metals, ceramics, recycling systems, and in situ resource utilization (ISRU) concepts. This review provides a comprehensive synthesis of AM technologies developed for space applications, with emphasis on demonstrated flight heritage, process behavior under microgravity and vacuum conditions, and materials validated in orbit. The paper surveys major AM process families relevant to space, including fused filament fabrication, directed energy deposition, ceramic stereolithography, bioprinting, and closed-loop recycling systems. Key ISS-based platforms—such as the Additive Manufacturing Facility, Ceramic Manufacturing Module, and Refabricator—are reviewed to assess technological maturity and system-level integration. Materials performance across polymers, metals, ceramics, and regolith-based feedstocks is discussed, highlighting the influence of microgravity, thermal transport, and environmental exposure. By comparing in-space results with terrestrial and reduced-gravity studies, this review identifies consistent trends, critical limitations, and remaining knowledge gaps, providing a structured perspective on the readiness of in-space additive manufacturing for future orbital and deep-space missions.</jats:p>

## pqac-00000020

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 4-6'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The Smith predictor control structure for powder blending combines PID control with RTD modeling to compensate for dead time in the process. The controller uses predefined RTD models to predict concentration at measurement time based on collected feeder speed data. The RTD prediction accounts for the delay caused by the system's dead time. A second model prediction of recent feeder speeds is made with dead time eliminated, allowing the controller to calculate error values that compensate for the process dead time. RTD is modeled using Tank-in-Series (TIS) models with parameters including dead time (tD), number of tanks (N), and mean residence time (τTIS). This advanced control structure enables better handling of dead time-dominated continuous powder blending processes compared to simple PID control.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 6-7'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The Smith predictor control structure for dead time-dominated powder blending uses residence time distribution (RTD) to compensate for process delays. The controller predicts feeder speeds using a model that eliminates dead time. The control input is calculated from the model error (difference between measured and predicted concentration) plus the predicted effect of actual feeder speeds, subtracted from the setpoint. RTD's cumulative distribution function (CDF) serves as the open-loop transfer function. This CDF is simplified to a first-order system by fitting a linear model characterized by simplified dead time (tDSM) and time constant (tCSM). The Smith predictor then subtracts the dead time from these time constants, making dead time an additional parameter determining control gains. This approach allows the controller to anticipate process dynamics and improve performance in systems dominated by significant transport delays.


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 11-12'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The Smith predictor control structure uses a residence time distribution (RTD) model of a dead time-dominated continuous powder blending system. The RTD model is mechanistically incorporated into the control structure, enabling it to function as a multipurpose tool essential in continuous manufacturing development. The dead time parameter serves as a key tuning variable that determines the proportional and integral gain settings. The control structure maintains the same basic parameters as a conventional PID controller but achieves improved performance by accounting for the system's dead time through the mechanistic RTD model. The Smith predictor demonstrated faster response to disturbances compared to PI control while maintaining stable concentration control, with proportional gains kept between 0.8-1.2 and integral gains near 0.03 for aggressive yet stable operation.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 1-2'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The study describes implementing residence time distribution (RTD) in a Smith predictor control structure for continuous powder blending. The Smith predictor uses RTD models to account for dead time-dominated system dynamics. A MATLAB GUI was developed to control powder blending using near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR) feedback and a volumetric feeder. The control system calculated and adjusted API concentration based on spectral data using both PID and Smith predictor approaches. The Smith predictor control structure was compared against classic PI control on systems with normal and amplified dead time. Results showed the Smith predictor reduced response time to disturbances by up to 50% and was less affected by dead time than PI control. The RTD models improved process design and provided wider control tuning flexibility. The Smith control structure proved more reliable and faster than conventional PI control for dead time-dominated processes.

## pqac-00000024

From text 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 2-4'.

Martin Gyürkés, Kornélia Tacsi, Hajnalka Pataki, and Attila Farkas. Residence time distribution-based smith predictor: an advanced feedback control for dead time–dominated continuous powder blending process. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, 18:1381-1394, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3, doi:10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3. This article has 4 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a Smith predictor control structure designed for dead time-dominated powder blending processes in pharmaceutical manufacturing. The Smith predictor was introduced to compensate for the destabilizing effects of dead time using a model-based control approach. The study couples the Smith predictor with the residence time distribution (RTD) of the powder blending process as the system model. RTD is used as the fundamental approach to access process dynamics and is integrated with the Smith predictor to create a mechanistic control structure. The RTD-based Smith predictor is compared against traditional PID control in the research. This control approach is presented as applicable and manageable for dead time-dominant process dynamics and extends beyond powder blending applications. The methodology represents an expansion of RTD models' applications in process design and active process control, addressing the challenge that many pharmaceutical continuous manufacturing processes are inherently dead time-dominated.

## 1. Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder
Valid Text Names:
'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 1-2', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 3-4', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 4-5', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 5-7', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 7-11', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 11-12', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 12-13', 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 13-13'

BibTex:
@article{fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof,
    author = "Fathollahi, Sara and Sacher, Stephan and Escotet-Espinoza, M. Sebastian and DiNunzio, James and Khinast, Johannes G.",
    title = "Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "AAPS PharmSciTech",
    volume = "21",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "8",
    issn = "1530-9932"
}


Abstract:
Highly potent active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and low-dose excipients, or excipients with very low density, are notoriously hard to feed with currently available commercial technology. The micro-feeder system presented in this work is capable of feeding low-dose rates of powders with different particle sizes and flow properties. Two different grades of lactose, di-calcium phosphate, croscarmellose sodium, silicon dioxide, a spray-dried intermediate, and an active ingredient were studied to vary material properties to test performance of the system. The current micro-feeder system is a volumetric feeder combined with a weighing balance at the outlet that measures feeder output rates. Feeding results are shown as a so-called “displacement-feed factor” curve for each material. Since the powder mass and volume are known in the micro-feeder system, in this work, we characterized an observed density variation during processing via a “displacement-feed factor” profile for each of the fed powders. This curve can be later used for calibrating the system to ensure an accurate, constant feed rate and in addition predicting feeding performance for that material at any feed rate. There is a relation between powder properties and feeding performance. Powders with finer particles and higher compressibility show densification during their feeding process. However, powders with larger particles and lower compressibility show both “densification” and “powder bed expansion,” which is the manifestation of dilation and elastic recovery of particles during the micro-feeding process. Through the application of the displacement-feed factor, it is possible to provide precise feeding accuracy of low-dose materials.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 11-12'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 25 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The displacement-feed factor is a relationship between piston displacement speed and powder feed rate that enables control of constant, low feed rates ranging from 1–20 g/h. The article demonstrates that density-displacement curves are highly reproducible but unique for each material, depending on powder properties. These curves remain independent of feed rate, allowing calibration of the syringe pump for accurate, consistent feeding. The study evaluated four excipient materials and two active ingredients, showing that the micro-feeder system achieved good repeatability and consistency. However, the excerpt does not provide specific RSD (relative standard deviation) values characterizing mass flow variability, though it does present flow rate deviations of 10–20% for some materials.

## pqac-00000027

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 14-18'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt addresses powder densification effects on feeding: when a hopper is refilled in volumetric mode, the feeder cannot compensate for increasing powder density as incoming material compresses existing powder, causing an increase in feed factor. Additionally, screw speed can suddenly change when the feeder switches to gravimetric mode after refill due to changed density. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is defined mathematically as SD divided by mean feed rate multiplied by 100 (equation 5). RSD is used to express variability in the feed rate, while relative error (RE) expresses deviation of mean feed rate from the setpoint. Four gravimetric feeding responses were obtained: RSD and RE at feed rate setpoints of 0.1 and 0.55 kg/h. The optimal hopper fill level (90% of FFmax) minimizes deviation from the feed rate setpoint during and after refill.

The excerpt describes the displacement-feed relationship in micro-feeder systems, indicating that feed rate equals relative effective displacement density (ρ*ED) multiplied by displaced volume per time, which remains constant. Powder densification significantly affects feeding rate: powders with larger particles and lower compressibility show initial higher feed rates with densification, while fine-particle, highly compressible powders show minimal densification during feeding. The feeding performance depends on particle size distribution, bulk density, compressibility, and elastic behavior. The excerpt mentions that relative effective displacement density (ρ*ED) and relative standard deviation (RSD) values for all materials are summarized in Table V, though the actual RSD values are not provided in these pages. This relationship demonstrates that controlling piston speed based on the measured densification profile can help manage feed rate changes.

The displacement-feed factor is a characterization tool used in micro-feeder systems to profile density variations observed during powder feeding. Since the powder mass and volume are known in the micro-feeder system, this factor can be used to calibrate the system for accurate, constant feed rates and predict feeding performance for materials at any feed rate. The relationship between powder properties and feeding performance is significant: powders with finer particles and higher compressibility show densification during feeding, while powders with larger particles and lower compressibility exhibit both densification and powder bed expansion (dilation and elastic recovery). The displacement-feed factor curve enables precise feeding accuracy of low-dose materials by accounting for these density variations.

## pqac-00000030

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 2-3'.

Sara Fathollahi, Stephan Sacher, M. Sebastian Escotet-Espinoza, James DiNunzio, and Johannes G. Khinast. Performance evaluation of a high-precision low-dose powder feeder. AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 25 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The micro-feeder system operates on a volumetric displacement principle where piston displacement in a cartridge controls powder feed rate. The instantaneous mass flow rate is calculated from volumetric displacement rate combined with density values. However, powder densification significantly affects feeding performance through two mechanisms: uneven powder distribution during cartridge filling and local densification caused by wall friction during feeding. These density variations directly impact the instantaneous mass flow rate, making consistency a critical challenge. To address this, the system introduces 'effective displacement density' (ΔV/Δm), normalized as 'relative effective displacement density' (ρ*ED) by dividing by tapped density, as an approximation of actual bulk powder density at the exit. The article emphasizes that minimizing density variations along the cartridge axis through appropriate filling procedures is essential for reliable manufacturing application.

From text 'fathollahi2020performanceevaluationof pages 3-4'.

AAPS PharmSciTech, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5, doi:10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5. This article has 25 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes how micro-feeder systems operate and how powder densification affects feeding rates. Powder densification significantly impacts the feeding process, creating four distinct phases: a starting phase with lag time due to powder compaction, an increasing feed rate phase as powder reaches saturated volume, a stable feed rate period suitable for steady feeding, and a process end phase when powder depletes. To minimize densification effects, a pre-conditioning procedure compacts powder to tapped density before feeding begins. The piston displacement is controlled at constant volumetric rate, with piston speed calculated based on powder amount and desired feed rate. Feed rates are measured by recording accumulated powder mass on a balance every second, with average rates calculated per minute. The document does not provide specific information about displacement-feed factor calculations or RSD (relative standard deviation) characterization values for mass flow variability.

## 1. Addressing Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Energy Management in Hybrid Vehicles: Comparing Genetic Algorithm and Sequential Quadratic Programming Within Model Predictive Control
Valid Text Names:
'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 1-2', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 2-4', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 4-6', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 6-8', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 8-9', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 9-12', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 12-13', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 13-15', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 15-17', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 17-18', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 18-20', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 20-22', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 22-25', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 25-26', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 26-28', 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 28-28'

BibTex:
@article{herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear,
    author = "Herkenrath, Ferris and Koßler, Silas and Günther, Marco and Pischinger, Stefan",
    title = "Addressing Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Energy Management in Hybrid Vehicles: Comparing Genetic Algorithm and Sequential Quadratic Programming Within Model Predictive Control",
    year = "2026",
    journal = "Energies",
    volume = "19",
    pages = "1535",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.3390/en19061535",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/en19061535",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "6",
    issn = "1996-1073"
}


Abstract:
<jats:p>Model Predictive Control (MPC) has emerged as a promising approach for energy management in hybrid electric vehicles, enabling predictive optimization of powertrain operation. The energy management problem in parallel hybrid powertrains constitutes a Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP) problem, combining continuous decision variables such as torque distribution with discrete decisions including engine on/off states and clutch engagement. This problem structure presents distinct challenges for different optimization approaches. Gradient-based methods such as Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) solve continuous, differentiable optimization problems and require auxiliary methods to handle integer variables, while metaheuristic approaches such as Genetic Algorithms (GA) can handle the mixed-integer structure directly at the cost of increased computational effort. This study presents a systematic comparison between GA and SQP as optimization solvers within an MPC framework for a P1P3 parallel hybrid powertrain. A multi-objective cost function is formulated to simultaneously optimize system efficiency, battery state of charge management, and noise emissions. Both approaches are evaluated across the WLTC as well as a real-world RDE scenario. On the WLTC, both MPC approaches reduce fuel consumption by 0.5–1.0% and improve system efficiency by 3.7–4.6% compared to a state-of-the-art deterministic reference strategy optimized for fuel consumption. At the same time, both approaches additionally achieve substantial reductions in noise emissions compared to the deterministic reference, which was not optimized for acoustic behavior. On both cycles, the GA-based MPC achieves favorable performance compared to SQP, with the performance gap widening from the WLTC to the RDE cycle. Both methods achieve real-time capability, yet SQP reduces computational time by a factor of four compared to GA. As long as computational resources in automotive ECUs remain constrained, this efficiency advantage positions gradient-based optimization for series production applications, whereas metaheuristic methods offer greater flexibility for concept development stages with relaxed real-time requirements. The findings contribute to the understanding of optimization algorithm selection for MINLP energy management problems in hybrid electric vehicles.</jats:p>

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000032

From text 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 54-57'.

Camilo Gonzalez Arango, Houshyar Asadi, L. Kooijman, and Chee Peng Lim. Neural networks for fast optimisation in model predictive control: a review. ArXiv, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668. This article has 51 citations.

The excerpt discusses neural network-based optimization (NNBO) methods for approximating MPC controllers for embedded applications. It presents a taxonomy of existing methods that classify frameworks by: (1) the type of MPC they apply to, (2) the types of guarantees offered, and (3) how the approximation is generated. The survey reviews approaches including explicit linear quadratic regulators for constrained systems, piecewise affine approximations for ultra-fast stabilization, and multiparametric programming algorithms. Key challenges mentioned include computing Robustly Controlled Invariant (RCI) or Robustly Asymptotically Invariant (RAI) sets for large nonlinear systems and solving large Mixed Integer Linear Programs (MILPs) or multiparametric Quadratic Programs (mpQPs). The excerpt emphasizes that from an application perspective, developing well-defined benchmarks is crucial for the field's advancement, while theoretical breakthroughs require stronger connections between control and learning theory.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 18-21'.

Camilo Gonzalez Arango, Houshyar Asadi, L. Kooijman, and Chee Peng Lim. Neural networks for fast optimisation in model predictive control: a review. ArXiv, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668. This article has 51 citations.

The excerpt discusses neural network approximation methods for MPC, specifically an explicit-implicit approach where a Deep Neural Network (DNN) optimiser is used to warm-start an active set solver. This preserves all MPC guarantees since a conventional solver still runs at each control iteration. The DNN learns primal variables for the prediction horizon using a Lagrangian-based loss function. Simple algebraic checks verify feasibility and suboptimality; if constraints are violated, the DNN output initializes the solver to find feasible optimal values. Simulations show significant speedup compared to cold-starting the solver. However, the excerpt notes this approach mainly benefits controllers already capable of real-time operation, as warm-starting from previous solutions is typical in subsequent iterations. The text also mentions probabilistic guarantee methods for LMPC providing 10x speedup on automotive hardware, though scalability concerns exist for larger problems.


---

## pqac-00000034

From text 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 39-42'.

Camilo Gonzalez Arango, Houshyar Asadi, L. Kooijman, and Chee Peng Lim. Neural networks for fast optimisation in model predictive control: a review. ArXiv, Sep 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668. This article has 51 citations.

The excerpt discusses neural network approximation methods for real-time MPC implementation, particularly for resource-constrained environments. Key approaches include: (1) imitation learning to train DNNs that approximate MPC controllers while achieving significant computational speedups (25-200x faster), enabling real-time execution; (2) validation frameworks with probabilistic guarantees that test DNN approximations on closed-loop trajectories to ensure constraint satisfaction and stability; (3) methods that preserve theoretical MPC properties by using DNNs to predict optimal costs as terminal costs in shorter-horizon reformulated controllers. These approaches trade-off safety guarantees for computational efficiency, with some methods tested on real hardware. The excerpt focuses on approximating robust MPC (RMPC) and multi-stage RMPC (msRMPC) formulations for nonlinear systems, demonstrating scalability to complex controllers while maintaining performance comparable to original controllers.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000035

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 1-2'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

The excerpt describes acados as embedded optimal control software implementing fast solvers for real-time applications. L4acados integrates learning-based models by incorporating external sensitivities through sequential quadratic programming (SQP) solvers. It enables Python-based residual models (such as neural networks and Gaussian processes) to be used in real-time MPC within acados. Key features include: (1) computing external sensitivities via user-defined Python modules, (2) supporting parallelization of sensitivity computations when preparing quadratic subproblems, and (3) supporting Real-Time Iterations (RTI). The paper demonstrates superior performance compared to available software, showing significant speed-ups and better scaling properties using neural-network-based control examples. The framework supports general Python-callable residual models while maintaining real-time constraints for embedded control applications, demonstrated through autonomous vehicle control examples.


---

## pqac-00000036

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 4-5'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

L4acados integrates learning-based models with acados for real-time optimal control by leveraging the acados Python interface to update model sensitivities during Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) iterations. The framework converts nonlinear OCPs by replacing nominal models with linearized discrete-time dynamics, enabling external residual models (such as neural networks or Gaussian processes) to be incorporated. Key features include: parallelized sensitivity computations across the prediction horizon using batch processing, support for Real-Time Iteration (RTI) strategy that splits computation into preparation and feedback phases, and flexibility to use various Python-callable learning models (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) or black-box models. Computationally, L4acados demonstrates significant speedups over L4CasADi approaches—with noticeable improvements already at prediction horizons N≥10, becoming more pronounced with increased model complexity. The framework maintains efficiency by performing sensitivity computations externally during RTI preparation, while acados handles remaining quadratic subproblem setup internally.


---

## pqac-00000037

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 2-3'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

L4acados is a framework that integrates learning-based models with the real-time optimal control software acados for embedded nonlinear MPC. The acados framework supports general Python-callable residual models, Real-Time Iterations (RTI), and parallelization of model and Jacobian evaluation during RTI preparation. L4acados specifically provides an efficient, modular implementation of Gaussian process-based MPC for real-time applications by extending the zoRO algorithm to the zero-order GP-MPC (zoGPMPC) setting. The framework supports arbitrary GPyTorch GP models and includes data processing strategies for online-learning applications. Computational efficiency is demonstrated through benchmarks comparing L4acados against other available software for learning-based MPC using neural-network-based residual models. The implementation is validated in both simulation and hardware experiments on an automotive miniature racing platform and a full-scale autonomous vehicle performing GP-MPC motion control for an ISO lane change maneuver.


---

## pqac-00000038

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 3-4'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

acados is a software package designed for embedded nonlinear model predictive control that provides fast OCP (optimal control problem) solvers and numerical integrators. It traditionally queries sensitivities of cost, dynamics, and constraint functions using symbolic expressions or direct user interfaces. L4acados integrates learning-based models with acados by providing a framework that incorporates external sensitivities into the SQP (Sequential Quadratic Programming) algorithm. It uses the acados Python interface to get/set model sensitivities at each SQP iteration, enabling real-time optimal control with learning-based models from PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. The framework treats linearized dynamics parameters (Âk, B̂k, ĉk) as model parameters within an affine dynamics OCP formulation, allowing SQP iterations to proceed using only evaluations at the current iterate rather than requiring full dynamics sensitivities. This approach recovers the exact same iterates as the original nonlinear problem under standard Hessian approximations.


---

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 8-10'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

The excerpt discusses L4acados, a framework for incorporating learning-based models (such as Gaussian processes) into the acados optimal control software. The paper demonstrates integration of learning-based models with real-time MPC control through experiments on autonomous vehicles. For computational performance, the authors show that their implementation achieves total computation times of 8.2 ± 1.4 ms for their zoGPMPC method, which is well within the 20ms control cycle time requirement for embedded real-time control. The framework allows external learning-based sensitivity information to be incorporated into optimal control solvers. The excerpt indicates that the GP-based MPC approach provides significant improvements over nominal MPC in control performance for vehicle path tracking tasks, including reduced actuator loads and more accurate path tracking while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for embedded deployment.

## pqac-00000040

From text 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 5-6'.

Amon Lahr, Joshua Näf, Kim P. Wabersich, Jonathan Frey, Pascal Siehl, Andrea Carron, Moritz Diehl, and Melanie N. Zeilinger. L4acados: learning-based models for acados, applied to gaussian process-based predictive control. ArXiv, Nov 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258. This article has 18 citations.

The excerpt discusses L4acados' integration of learning-based models with acados for real-time control. L4acados implements zero-order Gaussian process-based MPC (GP-MPC) by defining a GPyTorch residual model for efficient and parallelizable GP computations and extending acados' custom update function for uncertainty propagation using BLASFEO. Regarding computational performance, the excerpt shows that L4acados achieves improved scaling properties by computing sensitivities in parallel outside acados, unlike L4CasADi which exhibits linear scaling due to sequential sensitivity computation. The excerpt notes that acados' built-in OpenMP parallelization does not provide computational benefits when used with L4CasADi. However, the excerpt does not provide comprehensive information about all acados features for embedded nonlinear MPC or detailed computational performance metrics for embedded MPC applications.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000041

From text 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 1-2'.

Rafael Isaac Vásquez-Cruz, Ernesto Castellanos-Velasco, and José Fermi Guerrero-Castellanos. Frugal model predictive control and active disturbance rejection for laser beam steering systems. Control Theory and Technology, 23:513-528, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11768-025-00281-7, doi:10.1007/s11768-025-00281-7. This article has 3 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Frugal MPC (FMPC) is a control approach that combines model predictive control with active disturbance rejection by using a simplified integrator-chain model instead of a complex system model. This simplification enables a straightforward convex optimization problem to be solved at each sampling instant. The approach estimates total disturbances (which lump disturbances and model uncertainties together) using a discrete extended state observer (ESO). The estimated disturbance is then used in two ways: to handle system constraints in the quadratic optimization problem and as a feedforward injection to the plant for disturbance rejection, alongside the MPC feedback term. The key advantage is computational efficiency—FMPC is described as computationally economical, adaptable, efficient, and resilient, making it particularly suitable for embedded systems and applications with limited on-board computational resources.


---

## pqac-00000042

The Frugal Model Predictive Control (FMPC) approach integrates Model Predictive Control (MPC) with Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC) into a unified framework for laser beam steering systems operating under model uncertainties and external disturbances. The core methodology employs a simplified integrator-chain model combined with a discrete Extended State Observer (ESO) to achieve robust trajectory tracking while enforcing state and input constraints. This approach is specifically designed for embedded systems and real-time applications with limited computational resources. The FMPC demonstrates key performance characteristics including stable convergence to reference trajectories, strict constraint adherence for both actuators and outputs, and effective disturbance rejection. Notably, it accomplishes these objectives using a computationally economical quadratic programming formulation, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained environments. The framework bridges model-based optimization and model-free disturbance rejection paradigms, offering a balance between simplicity, adaptability, and resilience.

The frugal MPC (FMPC) approach reduces computational complexity while maintaining control performance through several key techniques. It uses a reduced control parametrization that introduces a parametrization matrix to decrease the number of decision variables during optimization, significantly lowering computational overhead. The system employs an Extended State Observer (ESO) that estimates both system states and external disturbances using the equation ẑ(k) = AOẑ(k −1) + BOu(k −1) + Ly(k), where observer gains depend on bandwidth. For the laser beam steering application, the FMPC is applied to an integrator-chain plant model (equation 21) to enable trajectory tracking despite disturbances and modeling uncertainties. The optimization uses gradient-based methods with saturated penalties rather than standard quadratic programming, allowing iterative solution with minimal computational overhead. This approach makes the controller compatible with real-time implementation on embedded systems while maintaining effective disturbance rejection capabilities.

The frugal MPC approach combines Model Predictive Control with an Extended State Observer (ESO) designed for fast embedded systems like laser beam steering systems with 5ms response times. The key innovation is a discrete-time ESO implemented as a predictor-corrector structure compatible with MPC. The estimated total disturbance is injected as a feedforward term while MPC provides feedback control. A control parameterization technique drastically reduces decision variables—achieving just two variables over a prediction horizon of N=10 with 1ms sampling time. The estimated disturbance is used to tighten input constraints online, preventing actuator saturation. The approach uses a piecewise constant disturbance model where ξ(k) is approximated as constant during each sample period. The entire algorithm is self-contained and includes a Gradient-Expansion iteration subroutine for real-time implementation. The framework captures model mismatches and external disturbances through an augmented plant model extended with disturbance dynamics.

The frugal MPC approach combines model predictive control with active disturbance rejection using an extended state observer (ESO) structure. The method employs a Proportional Integral Observer (PIO) to estimate and compensate for total disturbance signals online. The observer splits estimation into prediction and correction steps, similar to a discrete-time Kalman filter, allowing incorporation of current measurements. The MPC is designed based on a simplified linear model while tracking reference signals with zero steady-state error. The optimization problem minimizes tracking error and control effort subject to dynamic constraints, control saturation bounds, and rate limits. The estimated disturbance is actively compensated in the control law, with control constraints updated at each sampling instant based on disturbance estimation. This approach enables achieving MPC performance on embedded systems by using a simplified linear model while maintaining disturbance rejection capabilities.

## pqac-00000048

From text 'dey2603adaptivetubempc pages 10-11'.

Anchita Dey and S. Bhasin. Adaptive tube mpc: beyond a common quadratically stabilizing feedback gain. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.15912, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.15912. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt discusses adaptive tube-based MPC for handling parametric uncertainty and additive disturbances. In tube-based robust MPC, constraint tightening is used to guarantee robustness against disturbances. The tightening sets associated with disturbance bounds define the back-off from original constraints. In traditional approaches, these tightening sets remain fixed over time. However, the proposed adaptive method allows the tightening sets to shrink as the parametric uncertainty set contracts through online learning. This adaptive approach reduces conservatism by enlarging the region available for state trajectory evolution while still maintaining robust constraint satisfaction. The method integrates homothetic tube-based MPC with set-membership identification, allowing uncertainty sets to be updated online from state and input data. As uncertainty contracts, tube cross-sections adapt accordingly, leading to less conservative constraint tightening. This results in improved stabilization performance and reduced control effort compared to non-adaptive robust tube MPC schemes.

## pqac-00000049

From text 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 2-3'.

Alex Oshin, Hassan Almubarak, and Evangelos A. Theodorou. Differentiable robust model predictive control. ArXiv, Aug 2308. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426. This article has 29 citations.

Tube-based robust MPC augments a nominal controller with a feedback ancillary MPC that drives the true system state towards a nominal reference trajectory. The approach handles additive disturbances through constraint tightening: the nominal MPC operates on a "tightened" constraint set ¯X that is a subset of the true constraint set X. This ensures that even when the true system is subject to disturbances from unmodeled physics and noise that cause error between the true dynamics (ftrue) and model dynamics (f), the true state trajectory remains safe and does not violate the actual constraints. By keeping the nominal state sufficiently far from constraint boundaries, the method guarantees that disturbance-affected true states will still satisfy safety requirements. The excerpt does not explicitly provide the specific mathematical formulation of how back-off distance is calculated from disturbance bounds.

## pqac-00000050

From text 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 7-9'.

Alex Oshin, Hassan Almubarak, and Evangelos A. Theodorou. Differentiable robust model predictive control. ArXiv, Aug 2308. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426. This article has 29 citations.

Tube-based robust MPC is a control approach that maintains safety of a true system subject to disturbances by using two coordinated controllers: a nominal controller optimized for the deterministic case, and an ancillary (adaptive) controller that augments it for robustness. The method handles disturbances through constraint tightening of the state constraint set ¯X, which is parameterized and tunable. The tightening is achieved via differentiable barrier states (DBaS) in the cost function, with a penalty parameter qb that can be adapted online based on task performance and predicted system safety. The ancillary MPC tracks the nominal trajectory while compensating for disturbances, allowing the effective feasible region size to be determined through differentiable optimization rather than offline hand-tuning, enabling the controller to adjust its conservativeness based on encountered disturbances.

The excerpt discusses tube-based MPC approaches as established methods in the literature. Tube-based MPC handles additive disturbances through constraint tightening by using disturbance sets. The paper proposes an adaptive tube framework that improves upon standard homothetic tube MPC by updating the cross-sectional geometry of tubes online based on parameter estimates and uncertainty sets. Rather than using a single worst-case disturbance bound for constraint tightening, the adaptive approach leverages separate forward reachable disturbance sets for improved (less conservative) constraint characterization. The excerpt mentions characterizing disturbance sets Wt using system parameters and constraint sets, and computing forward reachable sets of the state over subsequent steps. However, the excerpt does not provide explicit details about how the back-off from constraints is computed based on disturbance bounds, though it indicates this is achieved through the disturbance set characterization methodology.

Alex Oshin, Hassan Almubarak, and Evangelos A. Theodorou. Differentiable robust model predictive control. ArXiv, Aug 2308. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426. This article has 29 citations.

Tube-based robust MPC is a control approach that handles model uncertainty and disturbances by dividing the online control problem into two optimization layers: a nominal MPC layer and an ancillary MPC layer. The nominal MPC generates a reference trajectory assuming no disturbances, while the ancillary MPC tracks this trajectory while compensating for bounded disturbances in system states. The approach guarantees that the true state remains within a bounded distance from the nominal trajectory, creating a "tube" around it. This framework accommodates bounded additive disturbances (w_t) from model uncertainty and noise while respecting control and safety constraints. The tube-based approach offers more flexibility than open-loop control through additional degrees of freedom while maintaining computational efficiency for real-time applications. However, the excerpt does not provide explicit details about how constraint tightening or back-off from constraints is calculated based on disturbance bounds.

Tube-based robust MPC is a control approach that uses homothetic tube structures to bound prediction errors and formulate constrained optimal control problems. The framework handles additive disturbances through constraint tightening—the predicted constraints are artificially tightened based on disturbance bounds to ensure robust constraint satisfaction despite model prediction errors. In conventional tube-based MPC schemes, tube geometry and constraint tightening are designed for worst-case uncertainty using fixed values. The excerpt indicates that constraint tightening reduces as uncertainty estimates improve through online parameter learning. However, the specific mathematical relationship between disturbance bounds and the back-off (tightening amount) from original constraints is not detailed in these pages, though the paper references existing homothetic tube MPC formulations ([20,27]) that presumably define these relationships.

The excerpt discusses tube-based robust MPC in the context of handling parametric uncertainty and disturbances. It explains that constraint tightening is used to manage additive disturbances, with tightening sets generated to account for disturbance bounds. The proposed adaptive approach generates time-varying tightening sets that improve the disturbance characterization at each step, whereas traditional robust tube MPC relies on fixed sets that conservatively account for all admissible disturbances. This results in smaller tube cross-sections with the adaptive method compared to fixed robust tube approaches. The paper demonstrates through numerical examples how tubes are generated and adapted over time, showing the polytopic shapes used to define homothetic tube cross-sections and how parametric uncertainty sets shrink through adaptation.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000055

From text 'stavrou2020assessingpowderflowability pages 36-40'.

Alexandros G. Stavrou. Assessing powder flowability at low consolidation stresses. Text, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.15126/thesis.00852876, doi:10.15126/thesis.00852876. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Powder cohesion is characterized as the shear stress when material is not subjected to an imposed normal load. It is typically determined by linearly extrapolating the yield locus towards low stresses, though this may be inaccurate for cohesive powders since the yield locus becomes increasingly curved at low stresses. The Warren Spring model characterizes the yield locus behavior. Measurement methods for powder flowability include the Compressibility Index (Carr Index) based on poured and tapped bulk densities, and the Hausner Ratio measuring the ratio of tapped to poured bulk density. Both are empirical tools for rough classification but have limitations as they depend on multiple particle and process parameters and do not measure fundamental bulk properties. The flow function is determined through shear cell testing, which yields unconfined yield strength and major principal stress values, enabling flow factor calculations for different pre-consolidation stresses. The effective angle of internal friction is derived from the steady-state Mohr circle tangent.


---

## pqac-00000056

From text 'pouliquen2025powdersandcohesive pages 9-12'.

Olivier Pouliquen. Powders and cohesive granular media: a rheological perspective. Rheologica Acta, 64:195-207, Apr 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00397-025-01490-2, doi:10.1007/s00397-025-01490-2. This article has 15 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Powder cohesion is characterized using the flow function technique, typically performed in a shear cell. The method involves compacting powder under maximum confining stress σmax, then reducing confinement to σi to create an over-consolidated state. The material is then sheared quasi-statically while measuring shear stress τ as a function of deformation. Peak yield stress (τyield) is measured at different confining pressures to create a yield curve, with the vertical-axis intersection providing cohesive stress (τcoh). The slope yields friction coefficient. Multiple yield curves at different preparation stresses reveal how cohesion varies with consolidation. The flow index (ffc), defined as the inverse slope of the unconsolidated yield stress versus consolidation stress relationship, quantitatively measures flowability, with ffc < 2 indicating very cohesive flow, 2-4 cohesive flow, 4-10 easy flowing, and >10 free flowing. Dimensionless numbers like the cohesion number (C = τcoh/σ) compare cohesive stress to typical confining pressure, with C > 1 indicating important cohesive effects.


---

## pqac-00000057

From text 'pouliquen2025powdersandcohesive pages 7-9'.

Olivier Pouliquen. Powders and cohesive granular media: a rheological perspective. Rheologica Acta, 64:195-207, Apr 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00397-025-01490-2, doi:10.1007/s00397-025-01490-2. This article has 15 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Powder cohesion is characterized through the cohesive shear stress (τcoh), which represents the yield stress in the absence of external loading due to particle adhesion. The cohesive stress relates to adhesive forces between particles: τcoh ∼ Fadh/d². Multiple measurement methods exist for determining cohesive stress and flowability: (1) flow through orifices of different sizes, where minimum diameter indicates tensile strength; (2) measuring powder overhang under gravity before breakage; (3) split tensile boxes that pull material apart; (4) inclined plane tests at critical flow angles for different layer thicknesses; and (5) linear or annular shear cells—the most widely used technique. Shear cells measure shear stress needed to deform material under varying confining stresses, constructing a yield curve. The flow function is a widely used technique developed in the powder community to characterize powder flowability and resistance to flow. Sample preparation significantly influences cohesive stress measurement, particularly overconsolidation effects.


---

## pqac-00000058

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 4-6'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

Powder cohesion is characterized through yield locus measurement, which quantifies stress required to cause powder failure. A ring shear tester applies normal consolidation force to a powder bed and measures tangential force needed for incipient flow. Using Mohr-Coulomb failure theory, major principal yield stresses are determined, and Flow Function Coefficient (FFC) is calculated as the ratio of these values. Research by Leung et al. analyzing 1130 ring shear measurements showed cohesion is the primary characteristic influencing FFC measurements, with particle friction coefficients being negligible. The excerpt also describes arching (or bridging), a phenomenon where cohesive forces between particles exceed gravitational forces, causing particle structures to form suspended structures in hoppers that resist powder displacement. ## pqac-00000063

From text 'herkenrath2026addressingmixedintegernonlinear pages 1-2'.

Ferris Herkenrath, Silas Koßler, Marco Günther, and Stefan Pischinger. Addressing mixed-integer nonlinear energy management in hybrid vehicles: comparing genetic algorithm and sequential quadratic programming within model predictive control. Energies, 19:1535, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/en19061535, doi:10.3390/en19061535. This article has 1 citations.

Mixed-integer MPC refers to Model Predictive Control applied to problems with both continuous and discrete decision variables. In hybrid vehicle energy management, the MINLP problem combines continuous variables (e.g., torque distribution) with discrete decisions (e.g., engine on/off states, clutch engagement). Different optimization solvers handle this structure differently: gradient-based methods like Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) are efficient for continuous problems but require auxiliary methods to manage integer variables, while metaheuristic approaches like Genetic Algorithms (GA) can handle the mixed-integer structure directly but with higher computational cost. The excerpt focuses on comparing these two approaches within an MPC framework for parallel hybrid powertrains but does not specifically address quantized inputs in MPC formulations.

## pqac-00000064

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 1-6'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

Powder feeding performance in loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders is measured through distinct metrics for volumetric and gravimetric modes. For volumetric feeding (uncontrolled), maximum feed factor (FFmax) indicates maximum feeding capacity, and feed factor decay (FFdecay) represents the optimal hopper fill level for refilling. For gravimetric feeding (controlled), two key accuracy metrics are used: relative standard deviation (RSD) measuring variability in feed rate, and relative error (RE) measuring the difference between the setpoint and mean feed rate. The actual feed rate is calculated from the difference in mass measured by the weighing platform divided by the time difference between consecutive measurements. The feeder's control system adjusts dispensing rate (screw speed) to minimize differences between actual and setpoint feed rates. The study indicates that RSD and RE serve as primary benchmarks for assessing gravimetric feeder performance, with RE validating whether the control system properly maintains accuracy across different powders.
