Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000070

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 4-6'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

Powder cohesion significantly impacts continuous direct compression feeding performance. Research demonstrates that cohesion is the primary characteristic influencing powder flowability, more influential than particle friction. High cohesion (adhesive stiffness values above 0.5 Kadh) causes arching or bridging—where cohesive forces between particles exceed gravitational forces, causing particles to form suspended structures in hoppers that resist powder displacement. This arching leads to inconsistent conveying screw feeding, improper filling of screw pitches, and irregular mass flow rates. Studies show increasing conveying speed has greater influence on particle velocity than altering cohesion levels. Colloidal silicon dioxide additives can mitigate cohesion effects by creating distance between particles and weakening van der Waals attractions, though effectiveness depends on carrier particle surface area and size. The excerpt indicates that managing cohesion through glidant loading based on powder physical properties represents a potential quality-by-design approach.


---

## pqac-00000071

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 3-4'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

Powder cohesion negatively impacts continuous direct compression (CDC) feeding performance by introducing variation through complex bulk behavior that results in inconsistent flow. High cohesivity inhibits flow, which is problematic for the feeding operation. Cohesive materials are prone to electrostatic charging, causing attraction-repellent behavior that leads to material deposits and adhesion to feeder surfaces. These buildups can migrate into the bulk feeder and dose it with spikes of material, resulting in compositional imbalance. The core difficulty of powder feeding involves converting larger volumes of powder in the hopper into smaller, consistent streams at the outlet, which is complicated when powder lacks flowability due to cohesion. The excerpt discusses that feeding is the first unit operation in CDC processes, so disturbances from cohesion can propagate downstream and impact tablet quality. However, the provided excerpt does not specifically address bridging, arching, or ratholing phenomena in screw feeder operation.


---

## pqac-00000072

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 1-3'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

This review article examines powder cohesion as a critical factor affecting continuous direct compression (CDC) performance. Powder cohesion is identified as one of the greatest obstacles to the CDC process because it inhibits powder flow. The article defines powder cohesion as the affinity of particles to adhere to each other, manifesting as resistance to powder flow and adhesive behavior. For CDC to function effectively, formulations require sufficient flowability to be transported through the system and adequate tabletability. Cohesion is noted as the most significant critical material attribute impacting pharmaceutical powder flow. The review consolidates literature on how cohesion and cohesion control measures affect three unit operations: feeding, mixing, and tabletting. Active pharmaceutical ingredients are identified as typically having very high cohesivity and poor flowability compared to excipients.


---

## pqac-00000073

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 27-28'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

The excerpt discusses how powder cohesion negatively impacts continuous direct compression (CDC) die-filling performance by reducing flowability. As cohesivity increases, critical velocity decreases, indicating poorer flowability and requiring cohesion control measures. The CDC process lacks a granulation step that would normally mitigate cohesion issues, so adequate flowability and tabletability are essential. Several mechanisms can improve die-filling of cohesive powders: higher paddle speeds in forced feeders allow cohesive powders to move more uniformly; introducing air provides lubrication effects and increases critical velocity; and suction filling reduces segregation and improves packing density. However, the effectiveness of these approaches depends on material properties, and active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) are noted as particularly cohesive and poorly flowing, presenting challenges for CDC processes. The excerpt does not discuss bridging, arching, or ratholing specifically.

## pqac-00000074

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 8-10'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

The excerpt discusses how powder cohesion affects continuous direct compression (CDC) feeding performance through several key relationships. Bulk density and flow factor consistency (FFC) are identified as common descriptors of powder cohesion that predict feeder behavior. Studies show that bulk density increases linearly with maximum feed factor, while tapped bulk density correlates with delta feed factor (the difference between maximum and minimum feed factors). Powders with FFC values greater than 3 achieve sub-1% flow rate variability, whereas low bulk density and low tapped bulk density powders exhibit large feed factor variations suggesting processing instability. Conditioned bulk density and compressibility at 15 kPa are strong predictors of minimum feed factor. The excerpt also notes that feed factors vary throughout hopper operation as gravitational compaction changes with fill level, and low hopper fill creates potentially inconsistent feeding. However, the excerpt does not directly address bridging, arching, and ratholing phenomena.


---

## pqac-00000075

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 10-12'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

This excerpt discusses the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (CDC) feeding performance. Cohesive powders worsen flowability and reduce the consistency of powder entering the conveying volume. This transient inconsistency results in variability in delivered mass flow and deviation from the desired setpoint. The authors emphasize the importance of ensuring cohesive powders are consistently motivated through the feeder's conveying section. Mitigation strategies include reducing interparticle contact, reducing triboelectric charging effects, refilling hoppers at moderate fill levels rather than low levels, and optimizing screw speed and screw type. The excerpt references multiple studies showing that hopper refilling frequency and technique significantly affect feeder stability, with gentler refilling at higher fill levels reducing maximum deviation and total mass deviation. However, the excerpt does not specifically address bridging, arching, or ratholing phenomena.


---

## pqac-00000076

From text 'jonessalkey2023reviewingtheimpact pages 24-27'.

Owen Jones-Salkey, Zoe Chu, Andrew Ingram, and Christopher R. K. Windows-Yule. Reviewing the impact of powder cohesion on continuous direct compression (cdc) performance. Pharmaceutics, 15:1587, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587, doi:10.3390/pharmaceutics15061587. This article has 36 citations.

The excerpt demonstrates that powder cohesion significantly impacts die-filling performance in tabletting operations. Higher cohesion reduces powder flowability, which negatively affects die-filling consistency. For good die-filling performance, powders need lower cohesive properties, including larger particle size, spherical particle shape, and lower surface energy, all of which contribute to improved flowability. Consistent die-filling is critical because mass and drug content uniformity depend heavily on die-filling performance; inconsistent filling impacts downstream quality attributes such as tensile strength and dissolution profile. The excerpt notes that cohesion, along with flowability, particle size, and morphology, are key bulk powder properties affecting die-filling performance. However, the excerpt does not directly address bridging, arching, ratholing, or screw feeder operation specifically.

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: paper_search

Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.

filter for minimum publication year.

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: We are designing a Model Predictive Controller for an open-source, low-cost gravimetric
powder doser and need a HIGH-EFFORT, citation-backed treatment of (a) which MPC
formulations fit this system and (b) — most importantly — exactly WHAT DATA we must
collect, and HOW, to identify the models the controller needs. A previous literature
review already covered the general landscape (decentralized PID vs. RGA vs. LQR/H-inf vs.
MPC; multi-objective scalarization; BO for outer-loop tuning), and concluded MPC is the
natural framework; do NOT repeat that survey — go deep and specific on MPC design and
system identification / experiment design for THIS class of plant.

THE PLANT. An Archimedean auger (screw) conveys powder from a hopper through a tube; a
stepper motor drives the auger (0-55 RPM, position-controlled, we command discrete
rotation increments or continuous speed); a solenoid tapper delivers discrete impulsive
taps (configurable pulse trains: on-time, off-time, duty, count) that knock loose powder
from the tube lip; a vibration motor provides continuous agitation; a servo tilts the
dispensing tube/plate (0-25 degrees used so far; steeper = faster flow); everything
dispenses into a cup on a balance read over serial. The balance is the ONLY process
sensor: ~1 mg resolution, needs ~0.8 s settling before a "stable" reading, so mass
feedback is slow, quantized, and noisy relative to a 0.1-5 mg/s flow. The controller runs
on a Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W host and/or an RP2040 (Pico) microcontroller.

EMPIRICAL BEHAVIOR ALREADY OBSERVED (real runs, one cohesive test powder):
- Feed factor is grossly powder-dependent and uncertain a priori: measured ~0.029 g/rev
  against a 0.5 g/rev default guess (17x off). An online per-rev estimator converged fine.
- Strong periodic per-rev yield alternation (e.g. 0.048 g then 0.006 g on successive
  half-rev increments) — consistent with the auger flight fill/dump cycle, i.e. a
  rotation-angle-dependent (cyclostationary) feed rate, not white noise.
- In-flight/falling powder: in a continuous bulk phase at ~0.13 g/s, halting the auger
  still let +162 mg land during the 0.8 s settling window — a transport delay / in-flight
  inventory that any anticipative stop must model (an `anticipation_g` margin knob exists
  but is untuned).
- Discrete tap yields: ~2-5 mg per 2-tap burst at 0 degrees tilt, but ~57 mg per fine
  cycle (half-rev + 2 taps) at 22.5 degrees — actuator effects interact strongly with
  tilt, and taps deplete the tube lip unless the auger "re-feeds" it between taps.
- Two controllers exist today: a two-phase (coarse auger to 95% + tap trim; 1 g in 201 s,
  -2.8 mg error) and a three-phase draft (continuous bulk -> half-rev fine -> tap; 2 g in
  21 s but +26 mg overshoot with untuned parameters). We want MPC to get the 21 s speed
  WITH the few-mg accuracy.
- Overshoot is asymmetric and effectively a hard constraint: powder cannot be removed
  once dispensed. Tolerance today ±5 mg; goal ±1 mg; stretch ±0.1 mg.
- Contexts that shift dynamics but are not controlled: powder identity (salt, xanthan
  gum, flour now; metal AM feedstocks like AlSi10Mg later), hopper fill level, ambient
  humidity/temperature and the powder's exposure history (cohesion, caking, bridging).
- A characterization sweep already runs on-device: at each tilt angle it measures
  per-rotation yield distributions, tap-only yield distributions (with re-feed
  accounting), and balance noise baselines, with repeats; results land in MongoDB keyed
  by powder_id.

PLEASE COVER, WITH CITATIONS:

1. MODEL STRUCTURE for MPC of screw/auger powder feeding. What model classes does the
   literature use for loss-in-weight (LIW) and screw feeders in pharmaceutical continuous
   manufacturing and powder filling machines: integrating (mass = integral of feed rate)
   models with input nonlinearity (Hammerstein), feed-factor models ff(fill level, screw
   speed), transport delay / dead time representations, cyclostationary or
   angle-dependent screw pulsation models, and stochastic flow-noise models for cohesive
   powders (avalanching). How is the in-flight (falling) powder column modeled — pure
   time delay, first-order lag, or explicit in-flight inventory state? How are discrete
   impulsive actuators (taps) modeled alongside a continuous screw — impulse response
   with depletion state (tube-lip inventory that taps drain and rotations refill)?

2. MPC FORMULATION. Given one integrating output, asymmetric hard constraint (no
   overshoot), a terminal target (batch/shrinking-horizon dosing rather than continuous
   regulation), and hybrid inputs (continuous screw speed + integer tap counts): compare
   shrinking-horizon batch MPC / minimum-time formulations, chance-constrained or
   tube-based robust MPC for the stochastic flow (what back-off from target does the
   theory prescribe given flow variance?), hybrid/mixed-integer MPC vs. treating tap
   bursts as quantized inputs vs. phase-switched MPC (bulk MPC then tap-trim policy),
   stochastic MPC with feed-factor uncertainty, and economic MPC trading dose time
   against terminal error. Which is appropriate at our scale, and what horizon /
   discretization (time-based vs. event/rotation-based sampling) does the literature use
   when the measurement itself needs 0.8 s settling?

3. STATE ESTIMATION with only a slow noisy balance: Kalman filtering of mass to estimate
   instantaneous flow rate and in-flight inventory (LIW feeders do exactly this during
   refill); handling measurement quantization (1 mg) and settling (should we use
   non-stable readings with a noise model instead of waiting for stable ones?); online
   feed-factor / g-per-rev estimation (RLS, EKF, observer with forgetting) and its
   interaction with MPC (indirect adaptive MPC); detecting regime changes (bridging,
   empty hopper, caking) as fault detection.

4. DATA COLLECTION / SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION — the core deliverable. Specify the
   experiment battery we should run, mapped to the model parameters above:
   - What excitation signals for the auger (steps, staircases, PRBS/multisine on screw
     speed, single-increment dosing pulses) and what sampling/repeat counts are needed to
     identify feed factor, its fill-level dependence, per-rev periodicity, dead time, and
     flow-noise spectrum, given 1 mg / 0.8 s measurement limits?
   - Impulse-response mapping for taps: burst size/count/duty sweeps, tube-lip depletion
     and re-feed dynamics — how many repeats for usable variance estimates?
   - Fill-level dependence: draw-down experiments (run hopper from full to empty logging
     mass vs. rotations) as done for LIW feed-factor maps?
   - In-flight inventory / stop-response tests: halt-from-steady-flow experiments at
     several speeds and tilts to identify the anticipation mass?
   - Cross-actuator interaction coverage: full factorial vs. D-optimal / space-filling
     designs over (tilt, speed, tap config, vibration) — what does optimal experiment
     design for control (identification for control, "identify what the controller needs")
     say about where to spend runs?
   - Environmental/context logging: what humidity/temperature instrumentation and
     metadata do powder-flow studies show actually matters?
   - How much of this must be re-done per powder vs. transferred/adapted online
     (multi-powder model libraries, transfer learning between powders)?
   - Concretely: recommended dataset schema — signals to log per run and per event
     (timestamps, commanded vs. actual rotation, tap events, mass traces including
     non-stable samples, contexts) so the same data serves ID, MPC validation, and later
     BO.

5. VALIDATION + PRACTICALITY: closed-loop vs. open-loop validation metrics for dosing
   (terminal error distribution, time-to-dose, constraint violation rate); how the LIW
   literature benchmarks feeder controllers; and implementable toolchains at our compute
   scale (explicit MPC lookup tables, offline-optimized profiles with run-to-run update,
   do-mpc / acados / CasADi / GEKKO on a Pi Zero class host). Finish with a recommended
   phased plan: which experiments first, which model first, which MPC variant first, and
   what quantitative evidence would justify each step up in complexity.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.
I'll create a comprehensive plan to address this detailed technical question about MPC design and system identification for a gravimetric powder doser.

## 2. Differentiable Robust Model Predictive Control
Valid Text Names:
'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 1-2', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 2-3', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 3-4', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 4-5', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 5-6', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 6-7', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 7-9', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 9-10', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 10-11', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 11-13', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 13-14', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 14-15', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 15-16', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 16-18', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 18-20', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 20-21', 'oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel pages 21-22'

BibTex:
@article{oshin2308differentiablerobustmodel,
    author = "Oshin, Alex and Almubarak, Hassan and Theodorou, Evangelos A.",
    title = "Differentiable Robust Model Predictive Control",
    year = "2308",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2308.08426",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2308.08426",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Deterministic model predictive control (MPC), while powerful, is often insufficient for effectively controlling autonomous systems in the real-world. Factors such as environmental noise and model error can cause deviations from the expected nominal performance. Robust MPC algorithms aim to bridge this gap between deterministic and uncertain control. However, these methods are often excessively difficult to tune for robustness due to the nonlinear and non-intuitive effects that controller parameters have on performance. To address this challenge, we first present a unifying perspective on differentiable optimization for control using the implicit function theorem (IFT), from which existing state-of-the art methods can be derived. Drawing parallels with differential dynamic programming, the IFT enables the derivation of an efficient differentiable optimal control framework. The derived scheme is subsequently paired with a tube-based MPC architecture to facilitate the automatic and real-time tuning of robust controllers in the presence of large uncertainties and disturbances. The proposed algorithm is benchmarked on multiple nonlinear robotic systems, including two systems in the MuJoCo simulator environment and one hardware experiment on the Robotarium testbed, to demonstrate its efficacy.

## 1. L4acados: Learning-based models for acados, applied to Gaussian process-based predictive control
Valid Text Names:
'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 1-2', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 2-3', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 3-4', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 4-5', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 5-6', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 6-7', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 7-8', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 8-10', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 10-10', 'lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels pages 10-11'

BibTex:
@article{lahr2026l4acadoslearningbasedmodels,
    author = "Lahr, Amon and Näf, Joshua and Wabersich, Kim P. and Frey, Jonathan and Siehl, Pascal and Carron, Andrea and Diehl, Moritz and Zeilinger, Melanie N.",
    title = "L4acados: Learning-based models for acados, applied to Gaussian process-based predictive control",
    year = "2026",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2411.19258",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.19258",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Incorporating learning-based models, such as artificial neural networks or Gaussian processes, into model predictive control (MPC) strategies can significantly improve control performance and online adaptation capabilities for real-world applications. Still, enabling state-of-the-art implementations of learning-based models for MPC is complicated by the challenge of interfacing machine learning frameworks with real-time optimal control software. This work aims at filling this gap by incorporating external sensitivities in sequential quadratic programming solvers for nonlinear optimal control. To this end, we provide L4acados, a general framework for incorporating Python-based residual models in the real-time optimal control software acados. By computing external sensitivities via a user-defined Python module, L4acados enables the implementation of MPC controllers with learning-based residual models in acados, while supporting parallelization of sensitivity computations when preparing the quadratic subproblems. We demonstrate significant speed-ups and superior scaling properties of L4acados compared to available software using a neural-network-based control example. Last, we provide an efficient and modular real-time implementation of Gaussian process-based MPC using L4acados, which is applied to two hardware examples: autonomous miniature racing, as well as motion control of a full-scale autonomous vehicle for an ISO lane change maneuver.

function.arguments: {"question": "How are loss-in-weight feeders modeled? What is the feed factor, how does it depend on screw speed and hopper fill level, and what models are used for the mass flow rate from screw feeders?"}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 6-10'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

Loss-in-weight (LIW) feeders are modeled using multivariate approaches that link material properties to process behavior. The feed factor represents the maximum feeding capacity for a given screw and material, determined during volumetric calibration. The feed factor varies as a function of hopper fill level—research introduced the 'feed factor profile' concept where feed factor is plotted against hopper fill level. Material properties (including bulk density and flow properties) correlate with the feed factor, with highly compressible powders showing feed factor decrease at higher hopper fill levels. The excerpt describes using PLS (Partial Least Squares) regression to correlate material property descriptors with both volumetric and gravimetric feeding responses. Volumetric mode maintains constant screw speed, while gravimetric mode controls mass flow rate. However, the excerpt does not provide explicit equations for mass flow rate modeling from screw feeders.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 22-26'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the development of multivariate PLS (Partial Least Squares) models to predict feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder. A four-component PLS model was created for FFmax (maximum feed factor), explaining 81.1% and 97.1% of variation in material properties and feeding responses. The feed factor depends primarily on density and powder flow properties. Feed factor shows strong dependence on hopper fill level, with FFmax occurring at 100% fill and decreasing as the hopper empties. Screw speed showed weak anti-correlation with FFmax (approximately -7%). FFdecay, describing the feed factor decrease to 90% of maximum, was modeled with three PLS components, explaining 55.8% and 80.9% of variation. This decay depends on powder compressibility, with higher compressibility leading to greater FFdecay. The models incorporate material property descriptors including density, flow properties, and compressibility to predict volumetric and gravimetric feeding responses.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 10-14'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the feed factor as a key metric for characterizing loss-in-weight feeder performance. The feed factor (measured in g/revolution) represents the mass of powder dispensed per screw revolution and is calculated from the feed rate and screw speed using equation (1). Feed factor profiles are generated by plotting feed factor against hopper fill level at different screw speeds (10%, 50%, and 90% of maximum). The excerpt demonstrates that feed factor depends on both screw speed and hopper fill level: at a given screw speed, feed factor is maximum at 100% hopper fill level and decreases as the hopper empties. Two key parameters are extracted from these profiles: FFmax (maximum feed factor indicating the feeder's maximum feeding capacity for a material) and FFdecay (the hopper fill level percentage where feed factor drops to 90% of FFmax, useful for optimizing hopper refill strategies). However, the excerpt does not provide explicit mathematical models for predicting mass flow rate from these parameters.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 14-18'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes aspects of loss-in-weight feeder operation relevant to feed factor behavior. The feed factor increases when incoming material compresses powder in the hopper, affecting the feeder's ability to maintain constant feed rates. During gravimetric mode operation after a hopper refill, screw speed changes based on the changed density. The optimal hopper fill level at 90% of FFmax was selected to minimize deviations from setpoint feed rate, as lower fill levels increase feed factor differences before/after refill while higher levels require excessive refilling. The study investigates how screw speed impacts two key volumetric feeding responses: FFmax (maximum feed factor) and FFdecay (feed factor decay), measured at three different screw speeds (6, 30, and 54 rpm). However, the excerpt does not provide explicit mathematical models for mass flow rate calculations or detailed modeling approaches for loss-in-weight feeders.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 1-6'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a multivariate modeling approach for low feed rate feeders. Partial least squares (PLS) regression was used to correlate feeding behavior with material properties. The study obtained two volumetric feeding parameters: maximum feed factor (FFmax) representing maximum feeding capacity, and feed factor decay (FFdecay) representing the optimal hopper fill level for refilling during gravimetric feeding. During volumetric feeding, screw speed is kept constant while the powder feed rate is not controlled. The models were developed using 25 material property descriptors from 15 pharmaceutical powders and validated on two new powders. The excerpt indicates that volumetric feeding responses were predicted better than gravimetric responses, confirming the feeder's control system effectiveness at low feed rates.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 2-4'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes loss-in-weight feeder operation and the feed factor concept, though it does not present explicit mathematical models for feeders. The feed factor is defined as the theoretically achievable mass flow rate at 100% motor speed. The mass flow rate from screw feeders is influenced by bulk density changes and hopper stress profiles. In gravimetric control mode, the control system adjusts screw speed to maintain target mass flow rate. The excerpt notes that hopper design significantly affects the solids stress profile inside the feeder, which in turn influences bulk density and mass discharge rate. During volumetric operation, mass flow rate depends on a predefined volumetric displacement rather than loss-in-weight measurements, making it sensitive to bulk density variations. The study investigated a redesigned hopper to homogenize stress in the hopper-screw interface zone and maintain constant stress level independent of fill height, thereby improving feed factor consistency during start-up and refill operations.

Abstract:
To support the 2024 MSOM Data-Driven Research Challenge, Merck & Co., Inc., Rahway, New Jersey (hereafter “MSD”), provides pharmaceutical manufacturing data from a continuous tablet production setting. The data set contains approximately 300 million data points related to around 75 process parameters monitored over 120 hours. In this paper, we present the data set and share our vision to inspire and facilitate new applications of operations management (OM) methodologies in pharmaceutical manufacturing. We begin with an introduction to pharmaceutical manufacturing for OM researchers and then elaborate on emerging technologies, common industry challenges, and research opportunities. We explain the data set and propose a roadmap for future research directions. Researchers are welcome to examine the proposed research questions or analyze other research questions using the data set. History: This paper has been accepted as part of the 2024 MSOM Data-Driven Research Challenge. Funding: This work was supported by The Dutch Research Council - NWO VIDI Grant. Supplemental Material: The online appendix is available at https://doi.org/10.1287/msom.2024.0860

## 1. Neural Networks for Fast Optimisation in Model Predictive Control: A Review
Valid Text Names:
'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 1-4', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 4-8', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 8-12', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 12-15', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 15-18', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 18-21', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 21-24', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 24-27', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 27-30', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 30-33', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 33-37', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 37-39', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 39-42', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 42-46', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 46-48', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 48-51', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 51-54', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 54-57', 'arango2023neuralnetworksfor pages 57-60'

BibTex:
@article{arango2023neuralnetworksfor,
    author = "Arango, Camilo Gonzalez and Asadi, Houshyar and Kooijman, L. and Lim, Chee Peng",
    title = "Neural Networks for Fast Optimisation in Model Predictive Control: A Review",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2309.02668",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2309.02668",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Model Predictive Control (MPC) is an optimal control algorithm with strong stability and robustness guarantees. Despite its popularity in robotics and industrial applications, the main challenge in deploying MPC is its high computation cost, stemming from the need to solve an optimisation problem at each control interval. There are several methods to reduce this cost. This survey focusses on approaches where a neural network is used to approximate an existing controller. Herein, relevant and unique neural approximation methods for linear, nonlinear, and robust MPC are presented and compared. Comparisons are based on the theoretical guarantees that are preserved, the factor by which the original controller is sped up, and the size of problem that a framework is applicable to. Research contributions include: a taxonomy that organises existing knowledge, a summary of literary gaps, discussion on promising research directions, and simple guidelines for choosing an approximation framework. The main conclusions are that (1) new benchmarking tools are needed to help prove the generalisability and scalability of approximation frameworks, (2) future breakthroughs most likely lie in the development of ties between control and learning, and (3) the potential and applicability of recently developed neural architectures and tools remains unexplored in this field.

## 1. Frugal model predictive control and active disturbance rejection for laser beam steering systems
Valid Text Names:
'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 1-2', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 2-3', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 3-5', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 5-6', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 6-8', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 8-9', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 9-11', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 11-13', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 13-14', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 14-15', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 15-16', 'vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive pages 16-16'

BibTex:
@article{vasquezcruz2025frugalmodelpredictive,
    author = "Vásquez-Cruz, Rafael Isaac and Castellanos-Velasco, Ernesto and Guerrero-Castellanos, José Fermi",
    title = "Frugal model predictive control and active disturbance rejection for laser beam steering systems",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "Control Theory and Technology",
    volume = "23",
    pages = "513-528",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1007/s11768-025-00281-7",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s11768-025-00281-7",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "3",
    issn = "2095-6983"
}


Abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This paper aims to fuse two well-established and, at the same time, opposed control techniques, namely, model predictive control (MPC) and active disturbance rejection control (ADRC), to develop a dynamic motion controller for a laser beam steering system. The proposed technique uses the ADRC philosophy to lump disturbances and model uncertainties into a total disturbance. Then, the total disturbance is estimated via a discrete extended state disturbance observer (ESO), and it is used to (1) handle the system constraints in a quadratic optimization problem and (2) injected as a feedforward term to the plant to reject the total disturbance, together with the feedback term obtained by the MPC. The main advantage of the proposed approach is that the MPC is designed based on a straightforward integrator-chain model such that a simple convex optimization problem is performed. Several experiments show the real-time closed-loop performance regarding trajectory tracking and disturbance rejection. Owing to simplicity, the self-contained approach MPC+ESO becomes a Frugal MPC, which is computationally economical, adaptable, efficient, resilient, and suitable for applications where on-board computational resources are limited.</jats:p>

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000006

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 7-10'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

During refill events, the LIW feeder control system switches from gravimetric (mass-based) to volumetric (speed-based) operation. The screw rotational speed is kept constant during refill, and feeding relies on volumetric displacement rather than powder weight loss measurement. This can cause densification of powder in the screws and lead to overfeeding. Once the refill ends, the gravimetric controller resumes control and adjusts screw speeds to achieve the target mass flow rate setpoint. Maintaining consistent bulk density during refill is critical since the system cannot rely on weight loss feedback during this phase.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 2-4'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

During refill operations, the LIW (loss-in-weight) feeder control system switches from gravimetric mode to volumetric mode because the hopper weight increases over time. In volumetric mode, the screw speed is set to a constant rate or pre-defined program rather than being adjusted by the control system. The mass flow rate estimation becomes dependent on predefined volumetric displacement rather than actual loss-in-weight measurements. This creates a significant challenge: any changes in the powder's bulk density directly influence the actual mass flow rate. Additionally, during refill, the feeder's load cell data becomes unreliable because it measures both the refill mass flow rate and the fed powder mass together, including dynamic scale responses, making it difficult to accurately monitor the actual dosing rate during this operational phase.


---

## pqac-00000008

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 1-2'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

During refill of a loss-in-weight (LIW) feeder, the control system switches from gravimetric to volumetric operation mode. In volumetric mode, the screw speed is set to a constant rate or pre-defined program rather than being adjusted based on load cell feedback. This occurs because the weight signal becomes unreliable during refilling as the hopper weight increases. Consequently, the mass flow rate is neither accurately known nor controlled during the refill phase. Additionally, refilling causes powder densification at the hopper-screw interface, which increases particle stresses and can result in overfeeding. The redesigned hopper in this study aims to mitigate these issues by homogenizing stresses and reducing sensitivity to refill-level variations.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 14-18'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

During hopper refill, the feeder operates in volumetric mode and cannot compensate for increasing powder density as incoming material compresses existing powder in the hopper, resulting in changes to the feed factor. When the feeder returns to gravimetric mode after refill, the screw speed can suddenly change due to the altered density of the powder. The excerpt indicates that selecting an optimal hopper fill level (90% of FFmax was chosen) is essential to minimize deviations from the feed rate setpoint during and after refill. A lower fill level results in larger differences in feed factor before and after refill, while a higher fill level requires more frequent refills.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'bostijn2019amultivariateapproach pages 10-14'.

N. Bostijn, J. Dhondt, A. Ryckaert, E. Szabó, W. Dhondt, B. V. Snick, B. V. Snick, V. Vanhoorne, C. Vervaet, and T. D. Beer. A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties. International Journal of Pharmaceutics, 557:342–353, Feb 2019. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066, doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066. This article has 89 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

During hopper refill, the LIW feeder operates in volumetric mode rather than gravimetric mode. In this volumetric operating state, the control system cannot compensate for changes in powder density that occur as incoming material compresses the powder already in the hopper. This inability to account for density changes causes the feed factor to increase unpredictably. When the feeder transitions back to gravimetric mode after the refill is complete, the screw speed can undergo sudden changes due to the altered density of the powder in the hopper. To mitigate these control issues and variability, the study notes that selecting an optimal hopper fill level for refilling is critical to minimize deviation from the intended feed rate setpoint.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 4-6'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

During refill, the loss-in-weight (LIW) feeder switches from gravimetric mode to volumetric mode. In this volumetric operation mode, the feeder's mass flow signal becomes unreliable because the feeder's scale records both the refilling weight and the weight loss from feeding simultaneously, making it impossible to distinguish between the two. Additionally, the powder's density changes in the screws as the powder weight in the hopper increases during refilling, which can lead to feeding inconsistencies and affect screw speed control. The excerpt indicates that refill data were excluded from performance evaluation and instead the dosed mass during refill was calculated from catch-scale data that was time-aligned with feeder data, rather than relying on the feeder's own measurements.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'fathollahi2024improvingcontinuouslossinweight pages 6-7'.

Sara Fathollahi, Valjon Demiri, Theresa R. Hörmann-Kincses, Snjezana Maljuric, Julia Massoner, Greg Mehos, and Johannes G. Khinast. Improving continuous loss-in-weight feeding accuracy by a novel hopper design. Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation, Sep 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2, doi:10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2. This article has 2 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The LIW feeder operates in gravimetric mode with moderate screw speed control. During refill operations, the feeder temporarily operates in volumetric mode. When refill portions are added to the hopper, increased compressive forces on powder at the hopper-screw interface cause densification (increase in powder bulk density) in the screws, leading to overfeeding because the system is in volumetric mode. After the refill ends, the gravimetric controller activates and adjusts screw speeds to compensate. The study found that higher refill portions and lower refill levels cause greater mass flow rate and feed factor deviations. More frequent refills at higher refill levels are recommended to minimize these deviations and control densification effects in the screws.

## 2. Residence Time Distribution-Based Smith Predictor: an Advanced Feedback Control for Dead Time–Dominated Continuous Powder Blending Process
Valid Text Names:
'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 1-2', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 2-4', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 4-6', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 6-7', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 7-8', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 8-11', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 11-12', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 12-13', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 13-14', 'gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased pages 14-14'

BibTex:
@article{gyurkes2023residencetimedistributionbased,
    author = "Gyürkés, Martin and Tacsi, Kornélia and Pataki, Hajnalka and Farkas, Attila",
    title = "Residence Time Distribution-Based Smith Predictor: an Advanced Feedback Control for Dead Time–Dominated Continuous Powder Blending Process",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "Journal of Pharmaceutical Innovation",
    volume = "18",
    pages = "1381-1394",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s12247-023-09728-3",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "3",
    issn = "1872-5120"
}


Abstract:
Purpose In continuous manufacturing (CM), the material traceability and process dynamics can be investigated by residence time distribution (RTD). Many of the unit operations used in the pharma industry were characterized by dead time–dominated RTD. Even though feasible and proper feedback control is one of the many advantages of CM, its application is challenging in these cases. This study aims to develop a feedback control, implementing the RTD in a Smith predictor control structure in a continuous powder blender line. Methods Continuous powder blending was investigated with near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR), and the blending was controlled through a volumetric feeder. A MATLAB GUI was developed to calculate and control the concentration of the API based on the chemometric evaluation of the spectra. The programmed GUI changed the feeding rate based on the proportional integral derivative (PID) and the Smith predictor, which implemented the RTD of the system. The control structures were compared even on a system with amplified dead time. Results In this work, the control structure of the Smith control was devised by utilizing the RTD of the system. The Smith control was compared to a classic PI control structure on the normal system and on an increased dead time system. The Smith predictor was able to reduce the response time for various disturbances by up to 50%, and the dead time had a lower effect on the control. Conclusions Implementing the RTD models in the control structure improved the process design and further expanded the wide range of applications of the RTD models. Both control structures were able to reduce the effect of disturbances on the system; however, the Smith predictor presented more reliable and faster control, with a wider space for control tuning.
